original_hypothesis.title: Mint/burn deadlock if MinDistributionPeriod is configured to 0 (supply becomes permanently frozen)
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: If MinDistributionPeriod is set to 0, then immediately after any distribution ends, `_isPastMinDistributionPeriod()` returns true in the same block ((block.number - LastDistribution) == 0 >= 0). `_beforeMintOrBurn()` then reverts, permanently preventing any mint or burn operations whenever there is nonzero supply and at least one holder. This can brick normal token supply operations.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a concrete configuration foot-gun that can permanently freeze critical admin and user actions. It is operationally plausible (0 used to ‘disable’ a feature) and should be guarded explicitly.
deployment_assumptions: MinDistributionPeriod is set to 0 at deployment or via a future contract version (no setter exists here)
There exists at least one holder and totalSupply() > 0
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: Constructor takes `_minDistributionPeriod` (LiquidInfrastructureERC20.sol constructor). Example script uses 10 (contract-deployer.ts), but no evidence for production values.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _isPastMinDistributionPeriod(): return (block.number - LastDistribution) >= MinDistributionPeriod; if MinDistributionPeriod==0 then always true when supply>0 and holders>0

preconditions: A distribution has occurred at least once (so LastDistribution is set and system is in steady state)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Time-gating logic uses >= without excluding zero period

operational_questions: Are there deployments with MinDistributionPeriod=0 (intentionally or by mistake)?
Is there any operational process that relies on post-distribution minting (mintAndDistribute)?
validation_requirements: Deploy with MinDistributionPeriod=0; mint to create supply/holders; attempt distribute then mint/burn in subsequent calls and observe deadlock

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: If deployed misconfigured, any actor can ‘trigger’ the deadlock effect by ensuring there is at least one holder and supply, then performing/causing a distribution.
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires misconfiguration or an intentional ‘0’ deployment choice.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Supply control permanently disabled; disapproved holders may be unable to burn if distribution gating engages
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Minting/burning workflows and possibly holder remediation (burning to exit)

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Repeated reverts 'must distribute before minting or burning' even immediately after distributions
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Owner unable to mint despite successful distributions

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
